coredata.dc:title: Experimental study of the effect of the state of charge on self-heating ignition of large ensembles of lithium-ion batteries in storage
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: He, Xuanze
Hu, Zhenwen
Restuccia, Francesco
Fang, Jun
Rein, Guillermo
coredata.dc:description: Self-heating can cause the ignition of open-circuit Lithium-ion batteries. Current safety literature focuses on the self-heating chemistry of a single cell, ignoring the effects of heat transfer. However, a large ensemble of batteries has a non-uniform temperature distribution and therefore self-heating ignition is dominated by both heat transfer and chemistry. This type of ignition is of importance when batteries are stored for long periods of time and in large ensembles but has been rarely studied to date. This paper studies the effect of the state of charge (SOC) on the self-heating behavior of LiCoO2 prismatic cells. The SOC of 0% (of interest in the safety of waste facilities), 30% (transport), 50% (storage), 80% (aged battery) and 100% (fully-charged battery), and 1, 2 and 4 cells stacked together were studied using oven experiments. Results show that cells at all SOC can self-ignite. Flames were only observed for SOC larger than 80%. We compare two temperature criteria: the temperature of the middle cell using the critical increase rate of 10 ℃/min defined in standard SAE-J2464, and the ambient temperature around the ensemble when triggering ignition. Both temperature criteria decrease with increasing SOC showing that the hazard grows with energy density. The cell temperature criterion is independent of the number of cells, while the ambient temperature criterion decreases as the number of cells increases, which indicates the increased risk of self-heating ignition when cells are stacked together in ensembles. Thus, the ambient temperature criterion should be used to design safe storage rather than the standard cell temperature increase rate, which does not represent well the criticality of ignition. The effective kinetics and thermal properties at different SOCs are extracted based on the Frank-Kamenetskii theory and are used to upscale laboratory results to storage conditions. The results in this work can improve the safety of the storage and provide scientific insight for safety standards.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ignition
Lithium-ion battery
Fire
Storage
Safety
Heat transfer

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the support from the China Scholarship Council (CSC) to XH and ZH, and the support from the Great Britain-China Educational Trust (GBCET) to XH. Many thanks to Yue Zhang (University of Science and Technology of China) for the help with the experiments.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
(a) The critical ignition 1-cell case of 100% SOC at Ta = 149℃, and (b) the no-ignition case of 100% SOC at Ta = 147℃. The battery ignition process has three stages, including preheating, self-heating and thermal runaway and fire, while the no-ignition process only has the first two stages.
T s
=
T a
should be used as the criteria of no-ignition.

The ignition phenomena of a 1-cell battery at 100% SOC with the oven ambient temperature of 149 ℃, (a) preheating leading to slight swelling, (b) electrolyte leakage from the cathode, (c) the occurrence of thermal runaway leading to the venting of gases, (d) the venting of intense projectiles after 2 s of thermal runaway, (e) mild venting of projectiles existing around 6–8 s, and (f) the intense fire after battery fell off.

The appearance of the different SOC cells after self-heating ignition. Thermal runaway occurs for 30% to 100% SOC cells, and only the 80% and 100% SOC cells can have a flame in the third stage leading to the deformation of the cells.

(a) The 1-cell cell surface temperature under time at different ambient temperatures. Self-heating causes a very small temperature increase. (b) The temperature rate of a 1-cell 0% SOC cell at Ta = 200℃.

The mass lost per cell at different SOC and their average mass loss rate (MLR) under the number of cells after experiments. The MLR of 80% SOC and 100% SOC cells are larger than those of 50% and 30% SOC cells, because of the flames after ignition.

(a) The voltage curve of the 100% SOC 1-cell case at Ta = 149℃ under time. The voltage has three drops during the self-heating ignition process, with the final drop indicating the ignition. (b) The cell surface temperatures in various conditions under different ambient temperatures when the 1st voltage drop occurs. This temperature is a unique material property, which is independent of the SOC and number of cells.

The cell thermal runaway temperature (
T
m , c
) using the criterion of 10 ℃/min of standard SAE J2464 [40] is independent of the number of cells, but increases as the SOC decreases.

The critical ambient temperature (
T
a , c
) triggering thermal runaway decreases as the number of cells increases, indicating the hazards of self-heating ignition of cells during storage. As the SOC increases, the
T
a , c
decreases.

The difference between the cell temperature and the ambient temperature criteria, under (a) number of cells and (b) SOC. Fig. 10 (a) shows that this temperature difference increases with an increasing number of cells. In Fig. 10(b), the negative R2 value means the best linear regression is a horizontal line, which proves that SOC does not explain the temperature difference and they are independent.

The Frank-Kamenetskii plot of cells at different SOCs. Linear fits are plotted to quantify the effective kinetics and thermophysical parameters, based on Eq. (4). The
R
2
values for fits of 30% to 100% SOC are 0.90, 0.99, 0.99 and 0.88, respectively.

Upscaling study of LIBs at different SOCs during storage using the parameters in Table 2, plotted in predicted critical ambient temperature under length of battery ensembles. The uncertainty is shown by the shaded regions based on the experimental errors. The upscaling geometry considers a LIB pile having a cross section of 1 m × 2 m and a variable length of L.
Different observations of cells at different SOCs in the 3rd thermal runaway stage.

row.entry: Not of interest due to a small temperature overshoot
Slight melting of plastic coating near the cathode.
Melting of the whole plastic coating strengthening its color.
Venting with smoke, sparks, and flare
Voltage: The 3rd time voltage drop to zero

head.ce:title: Experimental study of the effect of the state of charge on self-heating ignition of large ensembles of lithium-ion batteries in storage

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Fire Science, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Fire Science
University of Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Fire Science, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Imperial College London, London SW7 2AZ, UK
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Imperial College London, London SW7 2AZ, UK

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Engineering, King’s College London, London WC2R 2LS, UK
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Engineering, King’s College London, London WC2R 2LS, UK

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Self-heating can cause the ignition of open-circuit Lithium-ion batteries. Current safety literature focuses on the self-heating chemistry of a single cell, ignoring the effects of heat transfer. However, a large ensemble of batteries has a non-uniform temperature distribution and therefore self-heating ignition is dominated by both heat transfer and chemistry. This type of ignition is of importance when batteries are stored for long periods of time and in large ensembles but has been rarely studied to date. This paper studies the effect of the state of charge (SOC) on the self-heating behavior of LiCoO2 prismatic cells. The SOC of 0% (of interest in the safety of waste facilities), 30% (transport), 50% (storage), 80% (aged battery) and 100% (fully-charged battery), and 1, 2 and 4 cells stacked together were studied using oven experiments. Results show that cells at all SOC can self-ignite. Flames were only observed for SOC larger than 80%. We compare two temperature criteria: the temperature of the middle cell using the critical increase rate of 10 ℃/min defined in standard SAE-J2464, and the ambient temperature around the ensemble when triggering ignition. Both temperature criteria decrease with increasing SOC showing that the hazard grows with energy density. The cell temperature criterion is independent of the number of cells, while the ambient temperature criterion decreases as the number of cells increases, which indicates the increased risk of self-heating ignition when cells are stacked together in ensembles. Thus, the ambient temperature criterion should be used to design safe storage rather than the standard cell temperature increase rate, which does not represent well the criticality of ignition. The effective kinetics and thermal properties at different SOCs are extracted based on the Frank-Kamenetskii theory and are used to upscale laboratory results to storage conditions. The results in this work can improve the safety of the storage and provide scientific insight for safety standards.

ce:section.ce:para: The Lithium-ion battery (LIB) is one of the most important inventions of the last 30 years. Three inventors won a Nobel Prize in 2019 for their contributions to the development of LIBs, which have been widely used for consumer electronics, electric vehicles (EVs) and energy storage systems, due to their high power and high specific energy [1]. However, one vital drawback of these batteries is their tendency to ignite, resulting in a fire hazard. Although LIB fires are statistically rare, they have caused catastrophic consequences, led to economic losses and threatened the safety of users. For example, two LIB fires occurred in two Boeing 787 Dreamliners in January 2013 [2].

In the last 10 years, due to the increasing demand for LIBs for future transportation and renewable energy, how to prevent battery fires has become a highly studied research topic [3]. The understanding of thermal runaway at the material and cell levels has been widely studied [1]. The thermal runaway mechanism of a single cell and its prevention technologies have been thoroughly reviewed [4–6]. However, much less research attention has been given to the safety at the pack scale or even larger scale when cells are in storage or transport [1]. The causes of LIB fires of these large-scale conditions are still unknown because the knowledge developed for one cell cannot simply apply to large-scale conditions, due to the effect of heat transfer and the much more complex boundary conditions [7]. Unlike the fires of a single cell, in which the abuse conditions (mechanical, electrical and thermal) are the major causes, spontaneous ignition is the most common ignition type for large-scale conditions. For example, in terms of the EV fires in China, which has the world’s largest EV market, the most common cause is the suddenly spontaneous ignition (36.9%), followed by charging (26.2%) [1,8].

One cause of the spontaneous ignition of cells is the internal short circuit, which was thought to be the only cause of the spontaneous ignition for years [9], until an experimental study [10] proved that thermal runaway could be solely caused by internal chemical reactions without an internal short circuit. Another cause is self-heating ignition. Self-heating is the tendency of certain materials to undergo spontaneous internal exothermic reactions leading to an increase in their temperature [11–14]. Self-heating ignition can occur even at the ambient temperature for the large size of materials during storage, such as coal [15]. This is because when the sample size is relatively large, the heat generation rate that is proportional to sample volume is higher than the heat dissipation rate that is proportional to the sample surface, resulting in spontaneous ignition [11–13].

Self-heating has been reported as a common cause of battery fires in waste facilities[16]. The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) investigation on risks in transporting LIBs in cargo by aircraft also suggests that self-heating is the dominant cause of fire accidents of open-circuit batteries [17]. However, there are only a few works studying self-heating ignition, and the majority of current research focuses on a single cell. Tobishima and Yamaki [18] first conducted oven experiments to quantify the critical oven ambient temperature of self-heating ignition using a cylindrical LiCoO2 cell. After this, the effect of cathode chemistry [19], the state of charge (SOC) [20] and aging [21] have been investigated experimentally. A few numerical studies [22–25] have developed models for a single cell to help understand the chemical processes during self-heating ignition. They [22–25] conclude that the cathode reactions produce the dominated heat leading to a cell ignition. Some works [26–28] have theoretically predicted the critical self-heating ignition temperature using non-dimensional analysis based on Semenov theory, which assumes a lumped temperature within the cells. However, although these predictions [26–28] are accurate for one cell that normally has no temperature gradience as its Bi number is smaller than 0.1, they cannot be employed in large-scale open-circuit cells during storage, where their Bi numbers are much larger than 1 and therefore the effect of heat transfer has to be considered.

Ignition of a large-size open-circuit LIB ensemble is different to ignition of a single cell or a small module because the latter has a small Bi number and a uniform temperature distribution, where conductive heat transfer is not important [29]. Therefore, chemistry is the most important factor dominating ignition while heat transfer plays a secondary role. Comparatively, a large-size open-circuit LIB ensemble during storage has a non-uniform temperature distribution and a hot spot occurs at the center cell of the ensemble due to heat transfer and self-heating [30,31]. Ignition is dominated by both chemistry and heat transfer, which could lead to ignition occurring at the center cell while the ensemble outer surface temperature remains as ambient temperature [32]. Therefore, it is necessary and crucial to study large-scale LIB fires, however, which have not drawn much attention so far. He et al. [33] experimentally proved that when cells are stacked together, as the number of cells increases, the critical ignition ambient temperature decreases. This indicates that there is a self-heating ignition risk of thousands of cells during storage. A numerical study [34] of self-heating of a box LIBs shows that the heat generation from anode reactions plays an important role in the occurrence of ignition, which is different to the conclusions of one cell [22–25].

One of the dominant factors affecting the LIB chemistry is the SOC, which also significantly affects the ignition of cells across scales [20], because a cell with higher SOC has higher reactivity. Recently, Liu et al. [35,36] used two typical geometric-scaling experiments, which include oven experiment and hotplate experiment [11], to study self-heating ignition of LIBs at different SOCs. However, they found contradicting results. Their hotplate experiments [35] show that self-ignition is insensitive to the number of cells, which is against their oven experiment results [36]. It is necessary to conduct more experiments to justify what could be the reasons for this. The SOCs they selected were 30%, 80% and 100% SOC. The LIB fire risks in recycling bins (0% SOC) and storage conditions (50% SOC) were ignored. In addition, how the SOC affects the large-scale LIB storage condition has not been studied by considering the size and the SOC. Therefore, there is still a big knowledge gap in the ignition temperature of l large ensembles of LIBs, and it is worth further investigating the effect of SOC.

In this study, we first compare two self-heating ignition theories, Semenov theory and Frank-Kamenetskii theory, and point out Semenov theory should be used to analyze self-heating ignition of a single cell while Frank-Kamenetskii theory should be used to predict ignition of large storage conditions. We then conduct oven experiments to investigate the effect of the SOC using prismatic LiCoO2 LIBs. The 0%, 30%, 50%, 80% and 100% SOCs are selected respectively representing conditions of used batteries in waste facilities, shipping by aircraft, storage, aged batteries of the energy storage system, and the worst case of the energy storage system. The cell temperature and oven ambient temperature, voltage and time to ignition are measured. The experiments are not a connect to a practical storage condition, because only the number of cells from 1 to 4 is used. These laboratory-scale experiments aim to measure the effective kinetics and physical thermal properties of the cell. These parameters are further used to predict the ambient temperature triggering self-heating ignition of LIBs at real sizes based on Frank-Kamenetskii theory, where the critical ignition temperature is a function of the characteristic length of LIB ensembles. For the sake of simplicity, in this study we do not consider packing materials like insulation and cardboard. We refer to [34] for calculations of the heat transfer effects on ignition of these additional materials that are typical of storage conditions in warehouses and during transport. This study contributes to mitigating the fire hazards of cells during storage.

There are three theories that could describe self-heating phenomenon coupling exothermal reactions and heat transfer. The first theory is developed by Semenov [11–13], who presents the simplest mathematical formulation in a qualitative form assuming 1) a uniform temperature state of the system, 2) a one-step chemical reaction based on Arrhenius law, and 3) neglectable consumption of the reactant material. The Semenov theory is normally applied to describe the self-heating problem of gas or liquid, in which there is not a thermal gradience. The analytical result of the critical ambient temperature (
T
a , c
)
triggering self-ignition, the critical temperature of the fuel when ignition occurs, and a solution of time to ignition can be provided by the Semenov theory [11–13]. These results are independent of the fuel shape, but related to its size (the ratio of volume to the surface).

However, the assumption of uniform temperature distribution within the system limits the use of Semenov theory to solid, including LIBs. This is because only when the Bi number (
Bi =
h c
L / k , L
is the characteristic length, k is the thermal conductivity of the fuel,
h c
is the heat transfer coefficient at the boundary) of the solid fuel is smaller than 0.1 [37], the temperature gradience within the solid can be ignored. Therefore, the Semenov theory could only be used to analyze self-heating ignition of a single cell [26,27], but not the multiple cell conditions, such as battery module, pack or even cells in storage. The critical safe storage temperature obtained based on the Semenov theory is not proper [28].

In order to describe a realistic temperature distribution of a solid, the heat conduction of Fourier’s law within the solid needs to be incorporated [11–13]. Then, the energy conservation equation becomes:
(1)
k ∇
2
T + f
( t )
Δ
H c
exp
- E
RT
= ρ c
∂ T
∂ t

The boundary condition is
- k
∂ T
∂ x
=
h c
( T -
T a
)
, where T is the temperature of the fuel,
T a
is the ambient temperature,
f ( t )
is the mass action law that depends on the concentration of reactants at any time,
Δ
H c
is the effective heat of reaction of the fuel, E is the effective activation energy to describe the overall reaction, R is the universal gas constant, ρ is the density of the fuel, c is the heat capacity of the fuel, and t is time.

The theory to describe this transient heat conduction with a convective heat transfer boundary condition is known as the Thomas theory [11–13], which follows the assumptions of the Semenov theory, excluding the uniform temperature distribution. As the Thomas theory cannot provide an analytical solution due to the complexity of its partial differential equations, it is not commonly used in practical cases and the critical temperatures based on it can only be solved using numerical simulation.

The Frank-Kamenetskii theory is the most widely used theory for self-heating ignition prediction of solids [11–13]. It contains the assumptions of the Thomas model, and solving the Eq. (1) at the steady state in order to obtain an analytical solution. The steady state assumption is indeed a good approximation in practical cases as the temperature increase of the system before ignition is normally smaller than 20℃, where it could take weeks or even months [11–13]. The Frank-Kamenetskii theory could be used to analyze self-heating of LIBs when the steady state was reached before ignition [33], but should not be abused to the condition when the cell temperature before ignition was 70℃ higher that the oven ambient temperature [36].

The Frank-Kamenetskii theory defined a dimensionless heat generation number of δ to solve Eq. (1) at steady state [11–13], which is shown in Eq. (2):
(2)
δ =
E
L
2
f 0
Δ
H c
kR
T
a
2
e
- E / ( R
T a
)

where
f 0
is the value of mass action law at the initial time, which is constant as the consumption of materials is ignored. When analyzing self-heating ignition hazards of a material, the most important parameters in Eq. (2) are
T a
and L , because
T a
can vary from 0℃ to 40℃ and L can vary from centimeters to hundred meters, while other chemical and physical parameters remain the same for a certain material. The δ increases as the characteristic length L increases. Because self-heating reactions in a solid have a relatively large activation energy E
[11–13], and therefore δ increases as the ambient temperature
T a
increases.

During the process of derivation of Eq. (1), the Frank-Kamenetskii theory find when δ is higher than a critical value
δ c
, thermal runaway occurs leading to ignition [11–13]. The convective boundary condition of the Frank-Kamenetskii theory is dominated by the Bi number. When the
Bi =
h c
L / k
is large enough, the boundary condition becomes
T =
T a
, where the effect of
h c
is neglectable. In this case,
δ c
is only related to the geometry of substance. In terms of the prismatic cells we used, the
δ c
is shown in Eq. (3):
(3)
δ c
( a , b , c )
= 0.84
( 1 + 1 /
( b / a )
2
+ 1 /
( c / a )
2
)
where a, b, c is the half length of three sides with relation of a < b, c.

At super-critical ignition conditions, the dependence of critical ambient temperature can be obtained by rearranging Eq. (2) using the logarithm, as shown in Eq. (4):
(4)
ln
δ c
T
a , c
2
L
2
= ln
E R
f Δ
H c
k
-
E R
1
T
a , c

where
T
a , c
is the minimum ambient temperature where the ignition of the given sample size will occur.

The above analysis is based on the one-step overall Arrhenius reaction assumption. If the assumption is appropriate to be applied for LIBs, a straight-line correlation can be obtained by plotting the
ln
δ c
T
a , c
2
L
2
against
(
1
T
a , c
)
, which justifies that self-heating ignition of LIBs can be modeled by the Frank-Kamenetskii theory. The slope of the straight line is
-
E R
and the intercept corresponds to
E R
Â ·
f Δ
H c
k
, which helps quantify the effective kinetics and thermophysical parameters.

In the experiments, following our previous work, the Sanyo UF103450P prismatic cells produced in 2020 with the dimensions of 34 mm × 10 mm × 50 mm were used, because they are widely used in various commercial electronics and easy to be stacked together. The dimensions are the average integer values of the cells. The positive electrode of this cell is lithium cobalt oxide (LiCoO2), while the negative electrode is made of graphite. The cell has a nominal voltage of 3.7 V and a nominal capacity of 1880 mAh.

To investigate the effect of SOC, five SOCs were selected, including 0%, 30%, 50% and 80% and 100% SOC. 0% SOC represents the used cells placed in waste facilities, where self-heating of batteries is a major cause of the fires [16]. 30% SOC is the maximum SOC allowed when cells are shipped by aircraft according to the Packing Instruction 965 (UM 3480) by IATA [33]. 50% SOC is the recommended maximum SOC for cells during storage in a warehouse. 80% SOC is equal to the actual capacity of the aged cells at the end of their life, even if the cell is fully charged [38], while 100% SOC represents the most dangerous condition under normal operation for the energy storage system.

In sample preparation, in order to measure the actual electrical capacity and ensure the same SOC, each cell was cycled for three rounds at a 0.2C-rate for each charge or discharge process, with the final cycle to the designed SOC. Then, cells were rested at least for 5 h to avoid the effect of the internal temperature increase because of cycles. As each cell has a slightly different discharge capacity, we use 0.1C current to cycle 16 cells to obtain the average discharge capacity of 1930 mAh. The SOC is designed based on this average discharge capacity value. For example, in order to have a 30% SOC cell, we first fully discharge the cell and then charge a capacity of 579 mAh. The voltage values corresponding to different SOCs from 0% to 100% are 3.36 V, 3.78 V, 3.82 V, 3.99 V and 4.15 V, respectively.

The minimum critical ambient temperature (
T
a , c
)
is defined as the minimum ambient temperature that triggers thermal runaway leading to ignition. When carrying out an experiment, if cells failed to ignite, the experiment was repeated with fresh cells at a 5℃ higher temperature. If ignition happened, the experiment was repeated with fresh cells at a 5℃ lower temperature, until
T
a , c
was identified with the maximum error of ± 3℃. The measurement temperature error due to thermocouple is ± 1℃, and the error due to oven is also ± 1℃. Thus, the minimum critical ignition temperature has a 2℃-temperature range. The experiments corresponding critical conditions were repeated twice to minimize the experimental error.

The Bi numbers are calculated and presented in Fig. 1 (b). When the number of cells exceeds 2, the Bi greater than 0.1 and therefore the thermal resistance and the temperature distribution cannot be ignored anymore. In this case, the Frank-Kamenetskii theory (Bi greater than 0.1) should be used to analyze self-heating of LIBs rather than the Semenov theory (Bi < 0.1) [11,12]. Conduction heat transfer from the cell located in the center to the outmost cell surface includes the thermal contact resistance between cells and the thermal resistance within each cell. The contact resistance between two prismatic cells with polycarbonate coating is smaller than 0.7 K/W [39], while the conduction resistance is
δ
kA
= 5.44
K/W, which is about 8 times larger. Therefore, we can neglect thermal contact resistances without introducing any significant error.

Self-heating ignition phenomenon has three stages, including preheating, self-heating and thermal runaway. Fig. 2 presents the representative critical ignition and no-ignition cases of a 100% SOC cell, where the no-ignition case only has two stages. Cells failed to ignite at an ambient temperature of 147℃, but successfully ignited at an ambient temperature of 149℃ at 146 min. Therefore, the
T
a , c
of this case is 148 ± 1℃. Although the no-ignition case had an ambient temperature of only 2℃ smaller than the critical ignition temperature, the heat generation due to chemical reaction in proportion to ambient temperature and the sample size is still slightly lower than the heat dissipation. The cell temperature was always slightly higher than the ambient temperature during the self-heating stage, and then decreased to the ambient temperature at 230 min when the heat generation rate due to internal reactions is smaller than the heat dissipation rate.

In the first two stages, cells at different SOCs have very similar behavior, which has been summarized in the study [33] for 30% SOC cells. During the preheating stage, the cell temperature increased from the initial temperature to the ambient temperature, leading to slight swelling, which is different from the phenomenon of the 18,650 cells [36], where no obvious swelling was observed.

The criterion of the 2nd self-heating stage is when the cell temperature exceeds the ambient temperature. After that, the cell surface temperature remains slightly higher than the ambient temperature, with a temperature difference around 3-5℃ before 90 min, as shown in Fig. 2(a). The temperature difference then gradually increases until ignition occurs. The
T s
=
T a
is a critical self-heating phenomenon, which justifies whether the analytical solution of the Frank-Kamenetskii theory can be used. The temperature characteristics in the self-heating stage are different from the studies for cylindrical cells [36] and pouch cells [35], where they do not have a steady-state temperature stage. Even though the Frank-Kamenetskii theory was still used to analyze their data [36], large uncertainty was caused as their cell temperatures do not satisfy the boundary condition of the Frank-Kamenetskii theory. Moreover, during the self-heating stage, the electrolyte leakage occurred for all SOC cells leading to the gradual change of the surface coating color at the positive electrode from white to yellow. No obvious swelling was observed as the cell temperature was nearly unchanged.

In the 3rd stage, cells at different SOCs have significantly different behaviors. Table 1 summarizes the observations of cells at different SOCs in the thermal runaway stage. Only when the cell SOC is higher than 30%, self-heating ignition can occur showing a sharp temperature increase and the rapid swelling of the cell. 0% SOC cell can self-heating that leads to a temperature increase. At the critical ignition temperature, 30% SOC cells self-ignited without venting, but when further increasing the ambient temperature, venting would occur. Venting happened during thermal runaway of 50% SOC cells, releasing a mild jet of white gases. The ignition phenomena of 80% and 100% are similar and intense. Fig. 3 shows that the typical phenomena during self-heating ignition of 100% SOC cell at Ta = 149℃. Fig. 3 (c) presents that when thermal runaway occurred, a lot of gases were released in only 2 s, and no more gas venting was observed afterward. These gases were initially gathered on the top of the oven, and then filled in the whole oven gradually. Therefore, Fig. 3 (e) and (f) have a white background because of the poor visibility caused by the gases. Intense projectiles occurred at 8752 s, and this process only lasted 2 s. Due to the significant temperature increase to 700℃, tapes failed to fix the cell. Cell fell and the fire happened lasting around 25 s. Fig. 4 shows the appearance of the cells at different SOCs after self-heating ignition. Venting happened when the SOC was higher than 50%, and the color of its plastic coating was strengthened due to the melting. The flame occurred only for 80% and 100% SOC cells, where their plastic coatings were burnt out resulting in the deformation of the cell.

Fig. 5 (a) shows that the 1-cell experiments at 0% SOC at different ambient temperatures from 171℃ to 200℃. All cases show that there is a clear temperature increase due to self-heating. At Ta = 171℃, the temperature increase is 8.3℃, and this temperature difference further increases to 20.5℃ when increasing the ambient temperature to 185℃, but the temperature increase does not further increase even if the ambient temperature is higher. This is because the stranded energy of 0% SOC is too small. Even if the ambient temperature is higher, resulting in a faster reaction rate and temperature increase rate, the stranded energy and the reactants of 0% SOC are not enough to lead to a fast temperature increase but consumed quickly. Fig. 5(b) shows the temperature rate when Ta = 200℃, which is the fastest temperature increase rate in Fig. 5(a). The maximum temperature increase rate is only 12 ℃/min due to self-heating, which is larger than the thermal runaway criterion of 10 ℃/min in the standard of SAE J2464 [37]. In addition, there is an overshoot of temperature. No matter how small it is, this is indicative of self-heating. If there is self-heating, there is a point where self-ignition would take place. Therefore, we can conclude that 0% SOC can self-ignite but is not of interest for storage because the temperature overshoot is too small.

Fig. 6 shows the mass lost per cell and the mass loss rate (MLR) of cells at different SOCs. Similar to the previous study [36], the mass lost is insensitive to the number of cells but increases as the SOC increases. This is because, with higher SOC, more reactants are consumed during thermal runaway as the cell temperature is higher, and also more smoke and projectiles are vented out. In particular, 80% and 100% SOC cells have intense fires leading to much larger MLR than others.

Fig. 7 (a) shows the representative voltage curve of a 100% SOC cell at critical ignition temperature. The voltage characteristics of cells at different SOCs are very similar. Voltage has three drops during self-heating ignition, where the 1st drop occurs in the preheating stage dominated by the cell temperature, and the 2nd drop occurs in the self-heating stage and the 3rd drop indicates the ignition of the cells. We find that when the first drop occurs, for both critical ignition and no-ignition cases, the cell temperature is independent of either the SOC or the number of cells. The temperature of the cell is 133℃, as shown in Fig. 7 (b), which corresponds to the melting temperature of the PE membrane of the cell separator [41]. The 3rd voltage drop around 145 min is because ignition occurs leading to a short circuit, and the 2nd voltage drop is caused by an internal short circuit which has been previously justified in the literature [42], but we cannot directly verify it with our instrumentation setup. The time between the first voltage drop (initial failure signal) to the third drop (ignition) is relatively long. Once this temperature or the first voltage drop has been detected, there is enough time to react, therefore increasing the safety for both users and systems.

There are two essential temperature criteria for determining when thermal runaway occurs. One is the cell temperature criterion quantified using a critical temperature increase rate of 10 ℃/min as defined in standard SAE-J2464, and another is the critical ambient temperature triggering thermal runaway. The current technologies to estimate the LIB thermal safety using vent sizing package 2 (VSP2) [43], copper slug battery calorimeter (CSBC) [44] or accelerating rate calorimetry (ARC) [45,46] can only measure the cell temperature criterion, which is also the most commonly used temperature for LIB safety guidance [46]. However, due to the self-exothermic reactions, the ambient temperature triggering thermal runway away could be much lower than the cell temperature criterion. Therefore, it would be more interesting and important to know the critical ambient temperature, which actually should be the safety criteria for LIB.

Fig. 8 presents the cell thermal runaway temperature criteria of the middle cell using the criterion of 10 ℃/min from the standard of SAE J2464 [40]. This temperature is independent of the number of cells, which justifies the previous 1-cell ARC experimental measurement [45,46] to obtain this temperature is reasonable. The reason for this is that, despite the number of cells that could affect the heat dissipation, this effect is negligible when thermal runaway occurs. The heat dissipation is
S / V
h c
(
T m
-
T a
)
, where
h c
= 11 W/m2K in our oven [33]. The heat dissipation only counts to 16% comparing to the
c ρ d
T m
/ d t
when
d
T m
/ d t =
10 ℃/min. More importantly, when thermal runaway occurs, the heat generation is exponentially proportional to cell temperature. The increase of cell temperature by 1 ℃ would lead to the exponential heat generation increase overwhelming the effect of heat dissipation (16%). Therefore, the cell temperature criterion is solely dominated by its internal reactions, affected by cell SOC and state of health. In addition, as the SOC increases, the cell temperature criterion decreases, because higher SOC cells are much more reactive and contain more energy [4]. Specifically, the cell temperature decreases from 198 ℃ to 179 ℃, when the SOC increases from 30% to 100%.

Fig. 9 shows the critical ambient temperature triggering thermal runaway. Different to the
T
m , c
, we find that there are clear trends that the
T
a , c
decreases as the number of cells increases for all SOCs, because of the effect of heat transfer explained in the theory section. Although previous reviews [4–6] claimed thermal runaway of LIB occurs only if the cell temperature exceeds 175℃, we can find that when the number of cells is larger than 4, a much lower ambient temperature of 140 ℃ than expected could cause a fire hazard. The critical ambient temperatures quantified using oven experiments in Fig. 9 for 1 to 4 cells are much larger than normal storage condition ambient temperature. However, the
T
a , c
decreases as the number of cells increases. When thousands or even millions of cells are stacked together, the critical ambient temperature triggering thermal runaway is much lower than oven experiment results, even close to the possible storage ambient temperature around 40℃. The critical ambient ignition temperatures of real storage conditions are predicted in Section 5.2. We can also find that as the SOC increases, the
T
a , c
decreases. The
T
a , c
of 30% SOC and 50% SOC are much lower than those of 80% SOC and 100% SOC. Therefore, a recommended SOC of 30%-50% for storage and a compulsory maximum of 30% SOC for transport is appropriate.

For the first time, we also analyze the temperature difference between the
T
m , c
and the
T
a , c
, as shown in Fig. 10 . In Fig. 10 (b), we use linear regression to predict the dependence of this temperature difference on SOC. The R2 is −0.09 proves that the best linear regression is a horizontal line for the data. Therefore, this temperature is insensitive to the SOC, different from the trend of both the
T
m , c
and
T
a , c
. The temperature difference increases as the number of cells increases, as the
T
a , c
increases with the increasing number of cells, while the cell temperature criterion keeps unchanged. The temperature differences for 1, 2 and 4 cells are 28 ± 3℃, 31.5 ± 1.5℃, 35 ± 2℃, respectively. This is an interesting discovery and worthy more research in the future. We highly recommend using
T
a , c
as a safety reference temperature for LIB. But the measurement for the
T
a , c
using oven experiments costs lots of time and money. If we can find a theoretical prediction or even a correlation for the temperature difference, we could only measure the cell temperature criterion very quickly using a 1-cell ARC experiment, and then estimate the
T
a , c
for multiple-cell conditions for battery pack or storage.

Based on the Frank-Kamenetskii theory, only if the LIB boundary condition satisfies
T s
=
T a
, the analytical solution of the Frank-Kamenetskii theory shown as Eq. (2) to (4) can be used to quantify the effective kinetics and thermal properties. The boundary condition of
T s
=
T a
is a good approximation in this study based on the temperature experimental data of the self-heating stage, as shown in Fig. 2. Fig. 11 presents a Frank-Kamenetskii analysis plot of
ln (
δ c
T
a
2
/
L
2
)
vs
1000 /
T a
, based on Eq. (4). It can be seen that all SOC conditions have a linear fit, indicating that the assumptions of the Frank-Kamenetskii theory and the one-step reaction scheme can be applied for LIB self-heating ignition. Based on Eq. (4), the slope of the linear fit corresponds to
-
E R
, while the y-intercept is
ln (
E R
Â ·
f Δ
H c
k
). Therefore, the effective kinetics and thermal properties of cells at different SOCs can be extracted. Table 2 shows the value of these parameters and the R-squared values of linear fittings. The errors represent the highest and the lowest possible effective kinetics and thermal properties from the experimental data. The data we quantified helps predict self-heating ignition of LIBs.

When LIBs are stored in a warehouse, there could be more than 64 million cells [47]. Ignition of one cell could propagate the others leading to a catastrophic fire [48]. The large-scale LIB piles could lead to the relatively low
T
a , c
, which decreases with the increasing size of LIB piles. Therefore, it is important to the potential effect of size on self-heating ignition of LIB piles. In this section, the parameters in Table 2 and the effective conductivity k of 1.08 (W/mK) [49] for LiCoO2 cells are used to upscale the laboratory results based on the Frank-Kamenetskii theory, where Eq.(4) provides a calculation of the critical ambient temperature as functions of the number of cells and chemistry. This method has been widely used to predict the self-heating behavior of materials with complex chemistry [11–13]. The one-step effective kinetics quantified using the Frank-Kamenetskii theory also contains the effect of multi-step complex kinetics [13]. In addition, the predicted results based on the one-step effective kinetics would need validation when large-scale experiments are available. Currently, using theoretical prediction to show the trend and what could be the temperature value is a good scientific way in terms of LIB safety during storage.

The work [47] reported that, LIB piles have a cross section of 1 m × 2 m and another variable length of L in a rack during storage. Therefore, we select this geometry for our upscaling study. The ignition criterion still is
δ >
δ c
, which can be calculated using Eq. (2) and (3) respectively. Fig. 12 shows the 1D predicted temperatures of cells at different SOCs during storage. It is clear to see that the
T
a , c
decreases as the length of the pile increases, showing potential hazards of self-heating. Note that our prediction simply considers many cells stacking together, ignoring the complexity of packaging materials and contact thermal resistance. Nevertheless, the upscaling results could be instructive and useful for engineers and safety officers to design a safe plan for LIB storage.

Based on the report [47], the longest length of a rack in a warehouse is 30 m. At this length, self-heating ignition is unlikely to happen because even in terms of 100% SOC cells, the
T
a , c
is near 72 ℃. The suggested storage SOC is between 30% and 50%, where there also is not a self-heating ignition problem even the length of the pile reaching 1000 m. On the other hand, we have to keep in mind that these results are for LIBs without manufacturing defects. The self-heating certainly decreases the
T
a , c
for ignition, if there is a cell having manufacturing flaws or defects, a spontaneous ignition may still be possible. More importantly, the energy density of 100% SOC cell in this study is 180 Wh/kg, which is just the half value of current power cells for EVs. When the future cells contain even higher energy density, the ignition temperature might be even lower, indicating the risk of self-heating ignition of EVs or during storage. The time to ignition at typical ambient conditions up to 40 ℃ during storage could be from weeks to months. As LIB ensemble size increases, the time to ignition increases. However, using the Frank-Kamenetskii theory cannot predict time to ignition. The time to ignition could be calculated using computational models [32–34].

This study investigates the effect of the SOC on self-heating ignition of open-circuit LIBs using oven experiments. We find that SOC has negligible influence on the preheating and self-heating stages of LIBs, but significantly affects the ignition behavior. When the SOC is larger than 30%, self-heating ignition happens, and only if the SOC is larger than 80%, flames could occur. We observed three voltage drops during self-heating ignition. The cell temperature when the first drop happens is always 133℃ due to separator melting, which is independent of either the SOC and the number of cells. We compare two ignition temperature criteria, including the temperature of the middle cell
T
m , c
and the critical ambient temperature
T
a , c
triggering ignition. Both temperatures decrease as the SOC increases. But the
T
c , c
is independent of the number of cells, while the
T
a , c
decreases with the increasing number of cells, showing the hazards of self-heating ignition when thousands of cells are stacked together during storage. The temperature difference between these two temperatures is also independent of the SOC, but increases from 28 ± 3℃, 31.5 ± 1.5℃, to 35 ± 2℃ as the number of cells increases from 1, 2 to 4. This temperature difference could be used to estimate the
T
a , c
when only the cell temperature criterion is measured in some tests, such as the ARC. Finally, based on the Frank-Kamenetskii theory, the effective kinetics and thermal properties of cells at different SOCs are extracted. Using these parameters, we upscale our laboratory results to storage conditions, predicting the dependence of
T
a , c
on the number of cells during storage.

We suggest using the ambient temperature criterion to design safe storage rather than the standard cell temperature increase rate [40], which does not represent well the criticality of ignition. The critical ambient temperature of an ensemble containing LIBs decreases with increasing SOC and ensemble size, and we recommend using the Frank-Kamenetskii analysis to predict the safe storage size of the ensemble. This study extends our knowledge and deepens the understanding of the effect of the SOC on self-heating ignition of LIBs, contributing to the safety of open-circuit LIB storage and the improvement of current thermal abuse standards.

Xuanze He: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Zhenwen Hu: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Francesco Restuccia: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Jun Fang: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Guillermo Rein: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the support from the China Scholarship Council (CSC) to XH and ZH, and the support from the Great Britain-China Educational Trust (GBCET) to XH. Many thanks to Yue Zhang (University of Science and Technology of China) for the help with the experiments.
